NORTHROOT
ProductSystemsDocsJournalExamples

Section

ProductSystemsDocsJournalExamples
Journal

The `.nrj` Format Decision

Why Northroot starts with JSON and JSONL while keeping `.nrj` as an optional export container.

Northroot starts with JSON and JSONL because they are inspectable with ordinary tools, easy to diff, and enough for the first obligation, policy, event, receipt, and cost examples.

The .nrj format remains optional. It is justified only when a workflow needs properties that plain JSON and JSONL do not provide.

PropertyWhy it matters
Binary framingAvoid delimiter ambiguity in large exports.
Record kindsAllow readers to skip unknown frame types.
Streaming verificationVerify large audit exports without loading everything.
Truncation handlingDistinguish strict failure from permissive partial reads.
Cross-language stabilityKeep offline verifiers consistent across implementations.

Implementation Notes

If a workflow does not need those properties, it should use JSON/JSONL plus canonical hashes. .nrj should stay optional until tests and examples prove the extra format earns its keep.

Operational Lessons

The simplest inspectable format wins until the harder format earns its keep.

Source

northroot/docs/reference/nrj-decision.md

Explanations live here. Canonical contracts remain in source.
